paragraphs.text:
## 🔬 Metal–Protein Binding in NIST SRD-46

### Direct Answer: Intact Proteins Are **Not Present**

Searches for **transferrin, lactoferrin, serum albumin, and metallothionein** all returned **zero results**. NIST SRD-46 is scoped to small, well-defined ligands with critically evaluated thermodynamic constants — intact proteins are too structurally complex and heterogeneous to fit this framework.

paragraphs.text:
### What SRD-46 *Does* Cover: Biologically Relevant Small Molecules

The database has rich data for the **small-molecule building blocks** of metal–protein chemistry. Here is what is available for the three most biologically important metals:

| Metal | SRD-46 ID | Ligand Partners | Measurements (vlm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fe³⁺ | metal_61 | 406 | ~1,473 |
| Cu²⁺ | metal_41 | 1,951 | ~8,963 |
| Zn²⁺ | metal_208 | 1,169 | ~4,095 |

paragraphs.text:
### Fe³⁺ — Most Relevant to Transferrin Chemistry

Transferrin binds Fe³⁺ via **catecholate-like tyrosine residues + synergistic carbonate**. SRD-46 has excellent data for the small-molecule analogs of these binding modes:

| Ligand | Relevance to Transferrin/Siderophores | Data Points |
|---|---|---|
| Tiron (1,2-dihydroxybenzene-3,5-disulfonate) | Catecholate model | 16 vlm |
| Salicylic acid | Phenolate/carboxylate model | 15 vlm |
| Acetohydroxamic acid | Hydroxamate model (siderophore unit) | 10 vlm |
| Threoninehydroxamic acid | Amino-hydroxamate (siderophore model) | 12 vlm |
| Glycylglycylglycinehydroxamic acid | Tripeptide-hydroxamate (DFO model) | 11 vlm |
| Serinehydroxamic acid | Amino-hydroxamate | 11 vlm |
| NTA | Aminocarboxylate chelator | 16 vlm |
| EDTA | Strong hexadentate chelator | 14 vlm |
| CDTA | Strong hexadentate chelator | 14 vlm |
| Oxalic acid | Dicarboxylate (synergistic anion model) | 11 vlm |

paragraphs.text:
### Recommended Alternatives for Metal–Protein Data

| Resource | What It Offers |
|---|---|
| **MetalPDB** | Metal-binding sites in PDB structures |
| **BioLiP** | Protein–ligand binding (PDB-derived) |
| **UniProt** | Metal-binding annotations per protein |
| **PDB** (e.g. 1A8E) | Fe³⁺–transferrin 3D structure |
| **BRENDA** | Enzyme metal cofactor affinities |

paragraphs.text:
### 💡 Chemistry Insight

The hydroxamic acid ligands in SRD-46 (acetohydroxamic acid, threonine- and serinehydroxamic acids, glycylglycylglycinehydroxamic acid) are direct **structural units of natural siderophores** like desferrioxamine B — the iron-scavenging molecule produced by bacteria that competes with transferrin for Fe³⁺. The Fe³⁺–catecholate data (Tiron) similarly models the binding mode used by enterobactin, the strongest known Fe³⁺ chelator (log β ~ 49). SRD-46's small-molecule constants thus provide the thermodynamic foundation for understanding *why* transferrin (log K ~ 10²²) can hold iron so tightly — it mimics a tris-catecholate coordination environment.

Would you like me to retrieve the actual log β values for any of these systems (e.g., Fe³⁺–Tiron, Fe³⁺–acetohydroxamic acid, or Cu²⁺–histidine)?
